core_meaning: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> primarily means a physical 'thing' or 'object,' but it also works as a grammatical nominalizer — turning clauses into abstract noun-like concepts expressing expectations, emotions, or general truths. Think of it as either 'a thing (you can hold)' or 'the kind of thing that...'

sentences.jp: <ruby>床<rt>ゆか</rt></ruby>に<ruby>落<rt>お</rt></ruby>ちてる<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>、<ruby>全部<rt>ぜんぶ</rt></ruby><ruby>片付<rt>かたづ</rt></ruby>けといて。
sentences.translation: Pick up all the stuff that's fallen on the floor.
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> means physical objects; the casual contraction 〜ておいて→〜といて and dropped subject are natural in everyday speech.

sentences.jp: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べ<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>粗末<rt>そまつ</rt></ruby>にしちゃいけないよ。
sentences.notes: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べ<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> is a compound ('things to eat') — <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> forms many such compounds like <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>み<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>, <ruby>着<rt>き</rt></ruby><ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>, <ruby>建<rt>た</rt></ruby>て<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>というのは、すぐ<ruby>飽<rt>あ</rt></ruby>きるものだ。
sentences.translation: Children tend to get bored quickly — that's just how they are.
sentences.notes: 〜ものだ expresses a general truth or natural tendency; this is one of the most important grammatical uses of <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>頃<rt>ころ</rt></ruby>はよく<ruby>一緒<rt>いっしょ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>遊<rt>あそ</rt></ruby>んだものです。
sentences.translation: We used to play together often back in those days.
sentences.notes: 〜たものだ expresses nostalgic recollection of past habits — a distinct grammatical sense where <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> carries emotional nuance rather than referring to any object.

sentences.jp: <ruby>長年<rt>ながねん</rt></ruby>の<ruby>努力<rt>どりょく</rt></ruby>が<ruby>報<rt>むく</rt></ruby>われるというのは、<ruby>本当<rt>ほんとう</rt></ruby>にうれしい<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>ですね。
sentences.translation: It truly is a wonderful thing when years of effort are rewarded.
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> nominalizes the feeling into 'a thing that is deeply moving,' adding a reflective, slightly literary tone common in written or formal speech.

dont_use: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> refers to inanimate objects or abstract concepts; for people, use <ruby>者<rt>もの</rt></ruby>／<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby> instead — saying あの<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>は<ruby>親切<rt>しんせつ</rt></ruby>だ for a person sounds unnatural or dehumanizing. Also, don't confuse the grammatical 〜ものだ (general truth/nostalgia) with こと, which also nominalizes; they are not interchangeable. Avoid overusing the 〜ものだ pattern for opinions — it implies universal truth, so using it for personal preferences sounds odd.

confused_with.contrast: A common learner mistake is writing <ruby>泳<rt>およ</rt></ruby>ぐ<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>が<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きだ when the correct form is <ruby>泳<rt>およ</rt></ruby>ぐことが<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きだ. こと nominalizes actions or facts ('the act/fact of doing X') and is used after verbs in constructions like 〜ことができる, 〜ことがある, 〜ことにする. <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> nominalizes in a more emotional or nature-of-things sense (〜ものだ, 〜ものがある) — when in doubt with an action verb, こと is usually safer.

confusion_set.use_when: Talking about a physical object, or expressing general truths/nostalgia/natural tendencies with 〜ものだ
confusion_set.example: <ruby>大切<rt>たいせつ</rt></ruby>な<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>はちゃんとしまっておいてね。

confusion_set.use_when: Nominalizing an action or fact, especially in patterns like 〜ことができる, 〜ことがある, 〜ことにする
confusion_set.example: もっと<ruby>早<rt>はや</rt></ruby>く<ruby>起<rt>お</rt></ruby>きることにした。

confusion_set.use_when: Referring to a person (often in formal or written contexts, or fixed phrases like <ruby>若<rt>わか</rt></ruby>い<ruby>者<rt>もの</rt></ruby>)
confusion_set.example: <ruby>遅<rt>おく</rt></ruby>れた<ruby>者<rt>もの</rt></ruby>は<ruby>入場<rt>にゅうじょう</rt></ruby>できません。

frequency: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> is extremely common in both daily speech and writing. The physical 'thing' sense appears constantly in conversation; the grammatical 〜ものだ patterns are frequent in standard and written Japanese. Learners will encounter it at every level.
anki_hint: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> = a 'thing' you can hold OR a 'thing that is naturally so' — when it follows a plain-form verb, it's doing grammar (〜ものだ = that's just how it is), not pointing at an object.
